title: First Month Spending Plan
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: How can planning prevent financial regrets in your first month?
What balance between saving and spending feels sustainable for you?

stages.prompt: What should you do before spending your first income?

options.label: Spend first, plan later
options.reflection: While spontaneous spending might feel liberating, it often leads to regret and financial stress when bills arrive.

options.label: List expenses and savings first
options.reflection: Exactly! Planning before spending ensures your income covers necessities while building financial security.

options.label: Save everything and spend nothing
options.reflection: While saving is important, completely avoiding spending can lead to feeling deprived and missing reasonable opportunities.

options.label: Ignore planning and wing it
options.reflection: Flying blind with your finances often results in missed payments, late fees, and unnecessary financial anxiety.

stages.prompt: Which approach to budgeting works best for beginners?

options.label: Create a detailed budget with specific categories
options.reflection: Perfect! Detailed budgets help you understand exactly where your money goes and prevent overspending.

options.label: Make a rough estimate in your head
options.reflection: Mental estimates often lead to miscalculations and can cause you to overlook important expenses.

options.label: Rely solely on budgeting apps
options.reflection: While apps are helpful tools, understanding your own budget first builds better financial intuition.

options.label: Budgeting is too complicated for me
options.reflection: Basic budgeting is simpler than it seems and prevents the complexity that comes from financial disorganization.

stages.prompt: What's the most important category to prioritize?

options.label: Things that would be nice to have
options.reflection: Wants are important for quality of life, but they should come after ensuring your essential needs are met.

options.label: Entertainment and social activities
options.reflection: While fun is valuable, prioritizing entertainment over essentials can lead to difficult financial situations.

options.label: Luxury items and premium services
options.reflection: Luxury purchases should only come after establishing a solid financial foundation with needs consistently covered.

options.label: Essential needs like rent, food, and utilities
options.reflection: Exactly! Needs come first to ensure your basic living requirements are covered before discretionary spending.

stages.prompt: How much should you aim to save from your first income?

options.label: Save nothing until I have extra
options.reflection: Waiting to save often means never starting, as 'extra' money rarely materializes without intentional allocation.

options.label: Save half of everything I earn
options.reflection: While aggressive saving has benefits, saving half may be unrealistic and lead to feeling overly restricted.

options.label: At least 10-20% of your income
options.reflection: Excellent! This percentage builds an emergency fund while still allowing you to enjoy your earnings responsibly.

options.label: Start saving after I get a raise
options.reflection: Delaying savings formation often means missing the opportunity to build good financial habits early.

stages.prompt: What habit prevents first-month financial mistakes?

options.label: Forget about money until something goes wrong
options.reflection: Ignoring your finances until problems arise often means dealing with preventable stress and penalties.

options.label: Track spending daily and adjust as needed
options.reflection: Perfect! Daily tracking creates awareness and allows you to course-correct before small issues become big problems.

options.label: Follow the budget perfectly without exceptions
options.reflection: While discipline is important, rigid adherence without flexibility can lead to resentment and eventual abandonment.

options.label: Review finances once a month
options.reflection: Monthly reviews are helpful, but waiting a full month can mean missing opportunities to prevent problems early.
